\n

When the High Museum of Art in Atlanta launched Dutch Art in a Global Age in spring 2024, they faced a marketing challenge: how to draw modern audiences to an exhibition rooted in 17th-century Dutch art. The museum’s solution was as imaginative as it was effective—an entertainment-driven marketing campaign that turned citywide festivals into an extension of the museum experience.

\n

Through clever partnerships, interactive public art, and a deep understanding of Atlanta’s cultural pulse, the museum succeeded in making historic art relevant to new audiences. At the Museum Marketing Summit 2024, Madalina Anton, Senior Marketing Manager at High Museum of Art explained how they did it—and what other museums can learn from their approach.

\n

Rooted in Community, Built for Impact

\n

Founded nearly a century ago, the High Museum of Art has evolved from a small art collective into one of the Southeast’s leading cultural institutions. Its commitment to reflecting Atlanta’s diverse community drives its programming and visitor engagement efforts.

\n

In fiscal 2024, 51% of the museum’s visitors were BIPOC, and 51% were Atlanta residents—testaments to the institution’s efforts to be inclusive and welcoming. The museum’s initiatives, such as the annual Driskell Prize recognizing African-American artists and scholars, underscore its commitment to representation and social impact.

\n

This deep community connection became central to the museum’s marketing strategy for Dutch Art in a Global Age. The exhibition explored themes of global trade, colonial commerce, and artistic achievement—topics that resonate in a multicultural city like Atlanta.

\n
